questions.question: Explain the difference between fill rate and triangle throughput. How does each affect overall rendering performance?
keyPoints: Fill rate = pixels rendered per second (measured in GP/s)
Triangle throughput = triangles processed per second (measured in MT/s)
Fill rate bottleneck: high resolution, many pixel operations, overdraw
Triangle throughput bottleneck: high geometry complexity, many small triangles
Relationship: Both must be balanced for optimal performance

questions.question: What is memory bandwidth in GPU context? Why is it often the primary bottleneck in modern graphics applications?
keyPoints: Memory bandwidth = data transfer rate between GPU and VRAM (GB/s)
Bottleneck because: texture fetches, framebuffer writes, vertex data all compete for bandwidth
Higher resolution/textures = more bandwidth needed
Solutions: texture compression, render targets, tiling, cache optimization

questions.question: Define latency in computer graphics. How does it differ from frame rate, and why is low latency critical for VR applications?
keyPoints: Latency = time from input to visual feedback (motion-to-photon)
Frame rate = frequency of frame display
They are different: high fps can still have high latency
VR requires <20ms latency to prevent motion sickness
Components: input processing, GPU render, display refresh

questions.question: Compare forward rendering and deferred rendering in terms of their performance characteristics with multiple light sources.
keyPoints: Forward: lighting computed per object in single pass, O(objects × lights)
Deferred: geometry/lighting separated, O(pixels × lights)
Forward better for few lights and transparency
Deferred better for many lights (>16) but uses more memory for G-buffer
Deferred has fill rate limitations due to large render targets

questions.question: Explain what is meant by 'GPU utilization' and how would you diagnose whether the GPU or CPU is the bottleneck in a graphics application?
keyPoints: GPU utilization = % time GPU is actively rendering
Low GPU usage (<80%) + low FPS = CPU bottleneck
High GPU usage (>95%) + low FPS = GPU bottleneck
Tools: NVIDIA NSight, AMD RGP, RenderDoc, GPU-Z
Frame time analysis shows which pipeline stage dominates

questions.question: What is API overhead? How do modern low-level APIs like Vulkan and DirectX 12 reduce this overhead compared to OpenGL?
keyPoints: API overhead = CPU time spent on graphics API calls and driver management
OpenGL/DirectX11: heavy driver validation, state management on CPU
Vulkan/DX12: application manages GPU resources, minimal driver intervention
Benefits: better multi-threading, more predictable performance, reduced draw call cost
Trade-off: more complex application code

questions.question: A GPU has 8 GB VRAM and 256 GB/s memory bandwidth. A game runs at 4K resolution (3840×2160) at 60 fps. Calculate the minimum memory bandwidth required just for the color buffer, assuming 8-bit per channel RGB.
keyPoints: Resolution: 3840 × 2160 = 8,294,400 pixels
RGB 8-bit = 3 bytes/pixel
Required bandwidth: 497,664,000 × 3 = 1,492,992,000 bytes/s ≈ 1.49 GB/s
This is well under 256 GB/s, but actual requirements much higher with multiple render targets, textures, depth buffer, etc.

questions.question: Explain the graphics rendering pipeline stages and identify potential performance bottlenecks at each stage. Discuss optimization strategies for each bottleneck type.
keyPoints: Pipeline stages: Application → Vertex Shader → Tessellation → Rasterization → Fragment Shader → ROP → Framebuffer
Vertex shader bottlenecks: reduce vertex count (LOD), optimize vertex format, use instancing
Rasterization: simpler geometry, view frustum culling, backface culling
Fragment shader bottlenecks: reduce overdraw, optimize shaders, use lower precision
ROP/Framebuffer: reduce render targets, lower resolution, use compression
Memory: texture compression, mipmapping, cache-friendly access patterns

questions.question: You are designing a game for a mobile device with limited GPU performance. Describe the performance characteristics you need to consider, and propose rendering techniques suitable for maintaining acceptable frame rates on such constrained hardware.
keyPoints: Mobile GPU constraints: lower memory bandwidth, smaller caches, thermal throttling, less shader cores
Techniques: resolution scaling, reduced shadow map size, baked lighting instead of real-time
Tile-based deferred rendering (TBDR) - common in mobile GPUs
Simplified shaders, texture atlasing, reduced draw distances
Object culling, LOD systems, instancing for repeated geometry
Mobile-specific APIs: Vulkan, Metal, GLES3.1 with compute shaders

questions.question: What is the relationship between resolution, frame rate, fill rate, and memory bandwidth? Derive the mathematical relationship and explain why increasing resolution has a quadratic impact on fill rate requirements.
keyPoints: Basic relationship: Frame Rate = Fill Rate / (Resolution × Color Depth)
Fill Rate = Resolution × Color Depth × Frame Rate
Resolution = Width × Height (pixels)
When resolution increases by factor k, pixels increase by k²
Example: 1080p→1440p: 1.78× width, 1.78× height = 3.16× more pixels
Memory bandwidth scales similarly: more pixels = more data to read/write per frame
This is why 4K gaming requires ~4× the bandwidth of 1080p at same settings
